answer:
**The work is *Slokāntara* (unknown-slokantara-d7a628), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter illustrates the doctrine that "flaws and virtues arise from association" (*saṁsarga*) through the story of two parrots, **Gāvākṣa** (raised by a scholar) and **Girikā** (raised by a hunter). A king, separated from his retinue while hunting, arrives first at the hunter's house, where Girikā cries out:

> "Come on, come on! Smash his head!"[^2]

The king flees in fear and is then brought to the scholar's hermitage, where Gāvākṣa welcomes him politely:

> "Oh, fortunate are you, O King! My lord has deigned to visit the hermitage. Pray, rest, sir. Sit upon the new mat. Chew this betel nut and fresh betel leaf, with uncooked lime. If my lord is weary, pray, bathe, Your Majesty, in the spring."[^2]

The scholar explains to the astonished king that the parrots' contrasting behaviors stem from their associations.[^1]

[^1]: *Slokāntara*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-5797f7).
[^2]: *Slokāntara*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-slokantara-d7a628/01/translation/#p-5797f7).
